text:
GASTROINTESTINAL MEDICAL ONCOLOGY
ATTENDING ONCOLOGY VISIT NOTE -- TREATMENT TRANSITION / END-OF-INDUCTION ASSESSMENT

PATIENT: Raymond Kowalski
ATTENDING: [Attending Oncologist]
CC: Treatment transition planning following completion of 12 cycles of mFOLFOX-6 plus bevacizumab

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Mr. Kowalski is a 58-year-old Polish-American male with Stage IV (M1a) sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma with synchronous liver metastases (KRAS G12D mutant, pMMR/MSS) presenting today for end-of-induction oncology assessment following completion of his planned 12 cycles of modified FOLFOX-6 plus bevacizumab (Cycle 12 Day 1 administered approximately two weeks prior). Discussion today focuses on response assessment, toxicity review, and transition to maintenance therapy.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Mr. Kowalski was diagnosed approximately six months ago with Stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma presenting with synchronous liver metastases across bilateral hepatic lobes (four lesions total, largest 3.2 cm in segment VI). He underwent initial multidisciplinary tumor board evaluation and was deemed to have borderline resectable hepatic disease, warranting an upfront conversion chemotherapy approach with modified FOLFOX-6 plus bevacizumab with intent to reassess resectability following maximum response. His regimen was initiated at standard doses (oxaliplatin 85 mg/m2, leucovorin 400 mg/m2, 5-FU 400 mg/m2 bolus followed by 2400 mg/m2 over 46-hour continuous infusion, bevacizumab 5 mg/kg) on a biweekly schedule.

His induction course has been notable for the following:
- Partial response documented as early as Cycle 4 imaging, with continued deepening of response through Cycles 8 and 12.
- Cumulative oxaliplatin-induced peripheral neuropathy that progressed from Grade 1 (cold dysesthesias only, after Cycle 3) to Grade 2 (persistent rest symptoms, fine motor difficulty, after Cycle 7), prompting a 25% oxaliplatin dose reduction from Cycle 8 onward.
- Bevacizumab-associated hypertension, initially Grade 1 managed with up-titration of his home lisinopril, now stable at blood pressure 138/86 on lisinopril 20 mg daily.
- Mild anemia (Hgb nadir approximately 10.2-10.8), likely cumulative chemotherapy effect, without transfusion requirement.

End-of-induction imaging (CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast reviewed today) demonstrates a confirmed partial response to induction therapy:
- Sigmoid primary tumor reduced from 4.1 x 3.8 cm to 2.8 x 2.4 cm.
- Segment VI dominant hepatic lesion reduced from 3.2 x 2.8 cm to 1.6 x 1.4 cm.
- Three remaining liver lesions (Segments IVb, II, VII) at or near radiographic complete response (0.7 x 0.5 cm, 0.5 x 0.4 cm, and not definitively visualized, respectively).
- CEA has declined from 148.2 ng/mL at baseline to 22.8 ng/mL (an 85% reduction). CA 19-9 down from 312 to 89 U/mL.
- No new metastatic disease; no pulmonary nodules identified.

Today's visit focuses on the planned treatment transition. Per the OPTIMOX strategy, oxaliplatin has been discontinued given 12 cycles of cumulative exposure and Grade 2 peripheral neuropathy. The multidisciplinary hepatobiliary tumor board re-reviewed the case following Cycle 8 imaging and determined that, while the segment VI lesion remains identifiable, formal hepatic resection at this juncture carries undue risk given bilobar disease, estimated future liver remnant of approximately 28% (marginal), and concern for oxaliplatin-associated sinusoidal obstruction syndrome following the current exposure duration. A plan was made to transition to maintenance 5-FU/leucovorin plus bevacizumab (simplified LV5FU2 plus bevacizumab) and to reassess surgical consolidation options -- including possible staged hepatectomy versus radiofrequency ablation for residual lesions -- in approximately 3 months, contingent on neuropathy recovery and maintenance of imaging response.

Patient is here today for Cycle 1 of maintenance therapy (administration to follow this visit after labs confirmed). He is accompanied by his wife. He reports feeling reasonably well overall. His primary symptoms are ongoing tingling and numbness in his fingertips and toes (neuropathy, no longer as severe as it was during active oxaliplatin administration), mild fatigue, and occasional loose stools (1-2 per day, not requiring loperamide at this time).

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
Positive: Bilateral hand/foot paresthesias and numbness (Grade 2 neuropathy, improving slowly since last oxaliplatin), mild fatigue (Grade 1), occasional loose stools (1/day, no urgency or incontinence), mild intermittent nausea (no vomiting), mild scalp alopecia.
Negative: No fever, no chills, no chest pain, no shortness of breath, no hemoptysis, no rectal bleeding, no hematochezia or melena, no signs of bowel obstruction (regular bowel movements, no obstipation), no abdominal pain, no dysuria, no headache, no visual changes, no wound complications at port site. No edema. No significant weight change over the past 4 weeks.

---

PHYSICAL EXAM:
Vitals: BP 138/86 mmHg, HR 72 bpm, RR 16, temp 98.2F, SpO2 97% on room air, weight 176 lbs (BMI 27.1, slight weight loss from 178 lbs at start of induction, within expected range).

General: Alert, pleasant, well-appearing male in no acute distress. Mild alopecia noted. Well-nourished appearing.
HEENT: Anicteric sclerae. Mild oral mucositis not present today (no active stomatitis). Oropharynx clear.
Neck: No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs. No peripheral edema.
Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally, no wheezing or crackles.
Abdomen: Soft, non-distended. Mild right upper quadrant fullness without frank tenderness (consistent with known hepatic metastases). Spleen not palpable. Normal bowel sounds. No ascites. No palpable peritoneal masses.
Skin/Port: Right anterior chest wall port site clean, dry, intact, no erythema or signs of infection. Mild hand-foot skin changes consistent with Grade 1 hand-foot syndrome -- mild erythema of fingertip pads, no blistering, no significant pain.
Neurologic: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Decreased but improving vibratory sense in bilateral great toes (compared to prior exam -- objectively improved from worst point). Mild decreased light touch sensation in fingertips bilaterally. Gait intact, no ataxia. Fine motor tasks (button test) now performed without difficulty (improved from peak Grade 2 neuropathy).
Lymph nodes: No axillary or inguinal lymphadenopathy.

---

LAB RESULTS (reviewed today, drawn prior to clinic visit):
CBC: WBC 5.2 k/uL, Hgb 10.4 g/dL (Grade 1 anemia, stable), Hct 31.2%, MCV 88, platelets 210 k/uL, ANC 2.8 k/uL.
CMP: Na 138, K 4.1, Cl 101, CO2 26, BUN 14, Cr 0.88 mg/dL (GFR >60). Glucose 104 (diet-controlled DM, HbA1c not checked today).
LFTs: ALT 41 U/L (improving from 68 at time of progression assessment), AST 38 U/L, total bili 0.9 mg/dL, alk phos 112 U/L, albumin 3.8 g/dL.
Tumor markers: CEA 22.8 ng/mL, CA 19-9 89 U/mL (both down significantly from baseline, as above).
Urine protein:creatinine ratio: 0.14 (within acceptable range for continued bevacizumab; <2.0 is threshold for hold).
INR: 1.0.
Magnesium: 1.9 mg/dL (low-normal; continuing oral supplementation).

---

IMAGING RESULTS:
CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast (reviewed today, end-of-induction scan):
Sigmoid primary: 2.8 x 2.4 cm, reduced from 4.1 cm at baseline. Pericolonic nodes resolved. No bowel obstruction.
Liver: Segment VI lesion 1.6 x 1.4 cm (was 3.2 cm at baseline, 1.8 cm post-Cycle 8); three additional lesions at near-radiographic CR or not visualized. Segment VII lesion no longer definitively identified.
Lungs: No pulmonary nodules.
No new metastatic disease.
Best response: confirmed partial response. Approximately 52% reduction in sum of target lesion diameters from baseline.
CEA trajectory: 148.2 → 67.4 → 38.1 → 22.8 ng/mL (end-of-induction).

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

1. Stage IV (M1a) sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma, KRAS G12D mutant, pMMR/MSS, with liver-only synchronous metastases -- now with confirmed partial response to 12 cycles of mFOLFOX-6 plus bevacizumab.

Disease status: Mr. Kowalski has achieved a meaningful partial response to induction chemotherapy with 52% reduction in measurable disease burden and an 85% reduction in CEA from baseline. This is a favorable response, though full radiographic complete response has not been achieved, and the sigmoid primary tumor remains visible with residual viable tumor highly likely given partial response only. Hepatic disease is significantly improved but the segment VI lesion (1.6 cm) persists, and microscopic viability in the near-CR lesions cannot be excluded.

Treatment transition: Per pre-planned OPTIMOX-inspired strategy, oxaliplatin is being discontinued today after 12 cycles, given cumulative neurotoxicity (Grade 2, now improving) and completion of planned treatment course. Transitioning to maintenance simplified LV5FU2 plus bevacizumab (Cycle 1 Day 1 to be administered today following this note):
- Bevacizumab 5 mg/kg IV q2 weeks
- Leucovorin 400 mg/m2 IV over 2 hours
- 5-FU 400 mg/m2 IV bolus, then 2400 mg/m2 IV continuous infusion over 46 hours (pump)
Premedications: ondansetron 8 mg IV, dexamethasone 8 mg IV (reduced from induction dose now that oxaliplatin is off). Patient to be discharged home with 5-FU pump as usual. Prescription for ondansetron 4 mg PO q8h PRN nausea refilled.

Patient preference noted: Patient prefers infusional 5-FU over oral capecitabine given comfort with port access and familiarity with current regimen. This is medically reasonable and has been accommodated.

2. Surgical consolidation planning:
Hepatobiliary surgery has been re-engaged. Given maintained partial response and the segment VI hepatic lesion remaining the dominant concern, we will reassess surgical options after approximately 3 cycles of maintenance (approximately 6 weeks). At that point, neuropathy should continue to improve and we can reassess:
- Feasibility of R0 resection of segment VI lesion combined with ablation (RFA or microwave) of any residual smaller lesions
- Portal vein embolization to increase FLR if needed (currently estimated 28%, goal >30% for standard resection, goal >40% if significant hepatotoxicity from sinusoidal obstruction)
- Primary tumor resection timing: sigmoid colectomy to be planned once hepatic disease addressed (or concurrently if amenable to combined approach)
Patient understands potential for surgical consolidation and remains motivated for curative-intent approach. Referred to colorectal surgery for primary tumor operative planning.

3. Peripheral neuropathy (oxaliplatin-induced, Grade 2 at peak, now improving toward Grade 1):
Last oxaliplatin administered this cycle. Patient reporting subjective improvement -- fine motor function restored, rest paresthesias less prominent. Exam confirms improved but not resolved vibratory sense deficit. Expected to continue gradual improvement over 3-6 months following oxaliplatin discontinuation. Neurology follow-up appointment standing. Physical therapy for balance and proprioceptive exercises to continue. Will recheck neuropathy grading at next visit.

4. Hypertension (bevacizumab-related, Grade 1 currently):
BP 138/86 on lisinopril 20 mg daily. Acceptable control meeting target <140/90. Bevacizumab being continued in maintenance. Blood pressure monitoring at each visit and at home. Urine PCR 0.14 today -- acceptable. Will continue to monitor for proteinuria.

5. Mild anemia (Hgb 10.4, Grade 1):
Stable, not transfusion-requiring. Likely multifactorial: cumulative chemotherapy effect, possible chronic disease component. Iron studies to be checked at next visit to exclude iron deficiency as a correctable contributor. No ESA therapy indicated at this time.

6. Type 2 diabetes mellitus (diet-controlled):
Fasting glucose 104 today. HbA1c to be rechecked at 3-month interval. Diet counseling reinforced. Dexamethasone premedication dose being reduced now that oxaliplatin is off, which should help with glucose spikes on treatment days.

7. Psychosocial / goals of care:
Mr. Kowalski and his wife continue to demonstrate outstanding engagement with the treatment plan. He understands the nature of his disease -- that cure remains a goal but is not guaranteed and depends on achieving surgical resectability with adequate margins. He has been counseled that if surgical consolidation is not ultimately achievable, the disease remains incurable and future second-line therapy planning will become relevant. He is appropriately realistic while remaining hopeful and motivated.

Palliative care referral: To be placed as outpatient at this visit (patient has agreed). Will complement oncology care and assist with symptom management and anticipatory guidance.

Return to clinic: In 2 weeks (Cycle 2 of maintenance, Day 1). Will check CBC, CMP, tumor markers. Neuropathy and blood pressure reassessment at that visit.

Electronically signed,
[Attending Oncologist, MD]
GI Oncology Service
